text: The surgeon begins the gastric bypass procedure using the da Vinci XI robot by placing ports in a straight line directed toward the upper abdomen. An assistant port is placed just to the right of the umbilicus using a non-bladed optical entry trocar, followed by the placement of robotic operating ports, including a twelve millimeter robotic stapler port. The operation starts along the lesser curvature, creating a window into the lesser sac. The surgeon uses the EndoWrist stapler to form the gastric pouch over a forty French dilator, visible within the pouch for calibration. Seam guards are applied to part of the pouch, though they can shift and require repositioning. The robotic stapler's intelligent sensing aids in staple formation, and the pouch is completed without seam guards at the top of the staple line.

The surgeon identifies the transverse colon and uses a vessel sealer to divide the omentum, preparing for an anti-colic gastric bypass. The vessel sealer effectively divides the thick, fatty tissue of the omentum. The surgeon then brings up the loop approximately fifty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz and begins creating a side-to-side anastomosis. A gastrotomy is made in the pouch using the vessel sealer, which seals as it cuts, requiring manual opening. The endoverous stapler is used to create the anastomosis, with the third robotic arm assisting in positioning the small bowel and pouch for the gastrojejunostomy.

An NG tube is placed across the anastomosis to facilitate the closure of the enterotomy left by the stapler. The surgeon uses absorbable 2-0 VLOC suture to perform a running full-thickness closure of the defect, ensuring a tight suture line without gaps. The suture is then turned around to perform serosal stitches, burying the initial suture line and creating a second line in an open fashion.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to stabilize the bowel while creating enterotomies for the jejuno-jejunostomy using a vessel sealer. The vessel sealer cuts and seals the tissue, requiring stretching to open the sealed area. A white load stapler is used to create a side-to-side anastomosis, initially with a 45mm stapler, followed by a second firing to extend the anastomosis to approximately 90mm, ensuring a wide passage to prevent narrowing.

The surgeon closes the enterotomy in the small bowel using a silk crotch stitch at the end of the staple line, followed by a running full-thickness closure with absorbable 2-0 V-lock suture. The suture line is then reinforced with serosal stitches, creating a tight and secure closure. The same suture is used to close the mesentery defect, with the third robotic arm holding the mesentery in position for efficient stitching.
If you'd like any more information on this or other robotic procedures, check out my website at clarkgerhart.com.
